evals.prompt: You are in a session where a prose review earlier flagged: "sensory filter — 'Cael felt a warm streak of blood' — filtering distances the reader." The user now asks: "What did you mean by that filter thing?" Teach, following the lit-teach lesson shape.
evals.expected_output: A lesson anchored to THAT flagged line, not a generic lecture: locates filter words vs narrated perception; a working and a failing example; states the writer's question ('am I inside his head or watching him?'); runs a tinker micro-exercise on the flagged sentence (e.g. de-filtered variant offered as an option, not imposed); gives the caveat (filtering is deliberate POV-distance control in some voices, citing the detector-caveat pairing); ends with a small check-by-doing prompt.
expectations: The lesson explains the SPECIFIC flagged instance, pulling the line back up, rather than only defining the concept in the abstract.

All seven lesson-shape steps are recognizable: locate (incl. what it is NOT), working example, failing example, the writer's internal question stated explicitly, a tinker exercise on the user's sentence, the caveat/when-not-to-apply, and a closing check-by-doing prompt.
The de-filtered rewrite is offered as an option with reasoning, never imposed as the only correct form.

evals.prompt: You have the lit-teach skill loaded, including its intent-first diagnosis rule. The user writes: "She gave the city her youth, and the city, in return, gave nothing to her age — I don't know, something's off but I can't tell what I'm even doing here." Diagnose and teach.
evals.expected_output: Recognizes an attempted chiasmus/antimetabole (give-youth / give-nothing mirrored structure with near-symmetry), NAMES it, shows a completed canonical form of the figure, explains what breaks the symmetry in their version (the second limb's balance), and offers 1-3 repairs that finish the mirror — treating it as a device at 70%, never as a plain grammar or wordiness error.
expectations: The response names the attempted figure (chiasmus/antimetabole family) via intent signatures rather than flagging the sentence as generic awkwardness.
It shows what the completed figure looks like and pinpoints where the user's version loses the symmetry.
It offers concrete completion options and frames the attempt positively (device partially landed), never as a mere error to delete.

evals.prompt: You have the lit-teach skill loaded. The project's voice contract declares: 'protected device — sensory filter verbs (I hear it / I feel it) carry the narrator's dissociation; do not remove.' The user asks: "How do I cut filter words? I read they're bad." Teach.
evals.expected_output: Teaches the filter-word concept and the standard cutting technique honestly (the rule is real), AND surfaces the caveat prominently: THIS manuscript's contract protects filter verbs as a load-bearing dissociation device, so the technique applies elsewhere but not to the protected pattern; explains the POV-distance reasoning that makes both the rule and the exception coherent.
expectations: The lesson teaches the general rule accurately instead of refusing to teach it.
It explicitly cites the manuscript's voice-contract protection and tells the user NOT to apply the cut to the protected dissociation pattern, explaining why via POV distance.
The rule and the exception are presented as one coherent way of thought (distance control), not as a contradiction.

evals.prompt: You have the lit-teach skill loaded in the workflow repo. The user asks: "How do page-turn reveals work in manga pacing?" Describe how you prepare the lesson before teaching it.
evals.expected_output: Reads the medium-conventions research doc (docs/research/literary-pack/R2-mediums.md) manga section as ground truth rather than answering purely from memory; notes the medium inversion (edit against the page, not the paragraph); builds the lesson from that source with the page-turn question as the writer's question.
expectations: The response consults the committed R2 research doc for the manga section before teaching, per the knowledge-base rule.
The prepared lesson centers the page-turn reveal mechanic and the medium-specific way of thought (design against the physical page).
